probe_designs.content: Compute the positive prevalence p_k = mean(y_k) for each of the 40 attributes across train/val/test splits. CelebA is known to be heavily imbalanced (e.g., 'Bald' ~2%, 'Young' ~78%, 'Mustache' ~4%). Plot a bar chart of prevalences and flag attributes with p_k < 5% or > 95% as highly imbalanced. Check also that prevalence is consistent across splits (max |p_train - p_val| < 2%). Healthy signal: all 40 attributes appear in all splits with similar prevalence; the loss/metric strategy acknowledges imbalance (e.g., pos_weight in BCEWithLogitsLoss, or per-attribute F1 not just accuracy). Unhealthy: vanilla BCE with no reweighting + accuracy-only reporting, which hides collapse on rare attributes (model predicts all-zero for 'Bald' and still gets ~98% accuracy).
possible_sources: Liu et al., 'Deep Learning Face Attributes in the Wild' (ICCV 2015, CelebA paper)
Rudd et al., 'MOON: A Mixed Objective Optimization Network for the Recognition of Facial Attributes' (ECCV 2016)
He & Garcia, 'Learning from Imbalanced Data' (IEEE TKDE 2009)

probe_designs.content: Track per-attribute training loss and validation loss (and per-attribute AUROC / F1) across epochs. Aggregate mean BCE loss, but also examine the 40 individual curves. Healthy signal: validation loss decreases monotonically then plateaus close to training loss; per-attribute val AUROC > 0.85 on average with no attribute < 0.70. Unhealthy: large and widening train-val gap (train loss << val loss after a few epochs), or a subset of attributes (often rare ones like 'Blurry', 'Pale_Skin', 'Wearing_Necklace') showing val AUROC stuck near 0.5 while train AUROC climbs — indicating overfitting to majority class or memorization.
possible_sources: Goodfellow, Bengio, Courville, 'Deep Learning' (Chapter 7: Regularization)
Rudd et al., MOON (ECCV 2016) reports per-attribute error rates on CelebA
He et al., 'Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition' (CVPR 2016) on train/val tracking

probe_designs.probe_name: Per-Attribute Sigmoid Probability Calibration (ECE / Reliability Diagrams)
probe_designs.content: Since outputs are sigmoid probabilities thresholded at 0.5, calibration matters for downstream use. Compute Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and draw reliability diagrams for each of the 40 attribute heads independently (since BCE with imbalance often yields under/over-confident heads). Bucket predicted p into 10-15 bins and compare average confidence vs. empirical accuracy in each bin. Healthy signal: ECE < 0.05 per attribute; reliability curve close to the diagonal. Unhealthy: systematic miscalibration — for rare attributes the model outputs very low probabilities everywhere (peaked near 0), or the 0.5 threshold is far from the optimal Youden/F1 threshold, suggesting post-hoc temperature scaling or per-attribute threshold tuning is needed.
possible_sources: Guo et al., 'On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks' (ICML 2017)
Naeini et al., 'Obtaining Well Calibrated Probabilities Using Bayesian Binning' (AAAI 2015)
Nixon et al., 'Measuring Calibration in Deep Learning' (CVPR Workshops 2019)

probe_designs.probe_name: Demographic Subgroup Fairness (Male vs. Female, Young vs. Old, Skin Tone)
probe_designs.content: CelebA contains 'Male' and 'Young' attributes and has documented demographic skew (majority light-skinned, female-biased for some attributes). Split the test set by protected attributes (Male/Female, Young/Old, and if available Pale_Skin proxy) and compute per-attribute accuracy, TPR, FPR, and F1 on each subgroup. Compute disparity metrics: Demographic Parity Difference, Equal Opportunity Difference, Equalized Odds. Healthy signal: TPR gap and FPR gap across subgroups < 0.05 for each attribute. Unhealthy: e.g., 'Wearing_Lipstick' or 'Heavy_Makeup' classifier strongly gender-correlates — high TPR for females, near-random for males, indicating the model uses gender as a shortcut rather than the actual attribute.
possible_sources: Buolamwini & Gebru, 'Gender Shades' (FAT* 2018)
Hardt et al., 'Equality of Opportunity in Supervised Learning' (NeurIPS 2016)
Ramaswamy et al., 'Fair Attribute Classification through Latent Space De-biasing' (CVPR 2021)
Denton et al., 'Image Counterfactual Sensitivity Analysis for Detection of Unintended Bias' (CVPR Workshops 2019)

probe_designs.content: Measure inter-attribute correlation in the CelebA training labels (e.g., 'Wearing_Lipstick' ↔ 'Male' is strongly negatively correlated; 'Heavy_Makeup' ↔ 'Male' similarly; 'No_Beard' ↔ 'Male' strongly negative). Then evaluate the model on counter-stereotypical slices (males with lipstick, females with beards if available, or synthetically constructed via FairFace/CelebA-HQ). Healthy signal: attribute prediction accuracy does not drop sharply in counter-stereotypical slices. Unhealthy: >15% accuracy drop on these slices relative to stereotypical slices, suggesting the model latches on to spuriously correlated attributes instead of the causal features.
possible_sources: Geirhos et al., 'Shortcut Learning in Deep Neural Networks' (Nature Machine Intelligence 2020)
Sagawa et al., 'Distributionally Robust Neural Networks for Group Shifts' (ICLR 2020, GroupDRO)
Ramaswamy et al., 'Fair Attribute Classification through Latent Space De-biasing' (CVPR 2021)

probe_designs.content: Evaluate the classifier under realistic corruptions: Gaussian noise, blur, JPEG compression, brightness/contrast shifts, occlusion (random eraser), and face misalignment/rotation ±15°. Use an ImageNet-C-style pipeline adapted to faces. Compute mean attribute accuracy degradation per corruption type and severity. Also test small adversarial perturbations (FGSM ε=2/255, PGD 5 steps) for select safety-relevant attributes like 'Eyeglasses' or 'Wearing_Hat'. Healthy: <5% absolute accuracy drop on mild corruptions; graceful degradation with severity. Unhealthy: sharp collapse (e.g., 30%+ drop) under Gaussian blur or JPEG 10% quality, indicating over-reliance on high-frequency cues.
possible_sources: Hendrycks & Dietterich, 'Benchmarking Neural Network Robustness to Common Corruptions and Perturbations' (ICLR 2019, ImageNet-C)
Goodfellow et al., 'Explaining and Harnessing Adversarial Examples' (ICLR 2015, FGSM)
Madry et al., 'Towards Deep Learning Models Resistant to Adversarial Attacks' (ICLR 2018, PGD)

probe_designs.content: Accuracy is misleading for CelebA's imbalanced multi-label setting. Report a full metric suite per attribute AND aggregated: Balanced Accuracy, Per-attribute F1, Macro-F1, Micro-F1, Macro-AUROC, Macro-AUPRC, Mean Average Precision (mAP), Hamming Loss, Subset Accuracy (exact match), and Matthews Correlation Coefficient. Healthy signal: Macro-F1 > 0.65 and Macro-AUROC > 0.90 with no individual attribute AUROC < 0.70. Unhealthy: 90%+ 'mean accuracy' but Macro-F1 < 0.50 or several attributes with near-zero recall — classic symptom of majority-class prediction on imbalanced labels.
possible_sources: Zhang & Zhou, 'A Review on Multi-Label Learning Algorithms' (IEEE TKDE 2014)
Liu et al., CelebA paper (ICCV 2015) reports balanced accuracy per attribute
Wu et al., 'Tencent ML-Images' and multi-label evaluation best practices

probe_designs.content: Profile the timm backbone choice: parameter count, FLOPs, peak GPU memory during training, wall-clock training time per epoch, and inference throughput (imgs/sec, batch=1 and batch=32) at 224×224. Compare to established CelebA baselines (e.g., ResNet-50 reaches ~91% mean accuracy with known cost). Healthy signal: performance/compute is on or above the Pareto frontier of published baselines; gradient accumulation / AMP are used appropriately for backbone size. Unhealthy: model is many times more expensive than ResNet-50 baseline with < 1% absolute gain, or training is memory-bound and batch size is too small to estimate batch statistics (hurts BN-based backbones).
possible_sources: He et al., 'Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition' (CVPR 2016)
Wightman, 'PyTorch Image Models (timm)' library benchmarks
Tan & Le, 'EfficientNet' (ICML 2019)
Dehghani et al., 'The Efficiency Misnomer' (ICLR 2022)

probe_designs.content: For each attribute, generate Grad-CAM or integrated gradient saliency maps on correctly predicted samples and verify that the model attends to anatomically plausible regions: hair-related attributes ('Bald', 'Wavy_Hair') should highlight the top of the head; 'Smiling', 'Big_Lips' → mouth; 'Bags_Under_Eyes' → under-eye region; 'Wearing_Necktie' → neck/collar. Quantify using face-part segmentation (e.g., BiSeNet face parser) and compute IoU between top-10% saliency and the relevant facial region. Healthy: mean IoU with expected region > 0.3 and clearly higher than a random region. Unhealthy: saliency concentrates on background, image borders, or an unrelated region (e.g., 'Wearing_Necklace' attending to hair), indicating the model learned spurious cues.
possible_sources: Selvaraju et al., 'Grad-CAM: Visual Explanations from Deep Networks via Gradient-based Localization' (ICCV 2017)
Sundararajan et al., 'Axiomatic Attribution for Deep Networks' (ICML 2017, Integrated Gradients)
Adebayo et al., 'Sanity Checks for Saliency Maps' (NeurIPS 2018)
Yu et al., 'BiSeNet' face parsing (ECCV 2018)

probe_designs.content: CelebA attribute labels are crowd-sourced and known to be noisy/subjective (e.g., 'Attractive', 'Big_Nose', 'Oval_Face', 'Young' are inherently ambiguous). Detect label noise via (a) training a small model and flagging high-loss/high-confidence-disagreement samples with Confident Learning (cleanlab), (b) checking duplicate/near-duplicate identity leakage between train and test (CelebA has multiple images per identity — standard splits control this but verify), and (c) re-annotating a 200-sample subset and computing human-label agreement κ. Healthy: confident-learning estimated noise rate < 5% for objective attributes ('Eyeglasses', 'Wearing_Hat'), human κ > 0.7. Unhealthy: > 15% estimated noise on objective attributes, identity overlap across splits (same celebrity in train and test), or subjective attributes with κ < 0.4 — in which case treat those attributes as aspirational rather than ground truth.
possible_sources: Northcutt et al., 'Confident Learning: Estimating Uncertainty in Dataset Labels' (JAIR 2021)
Northcutt, Athalye, Mueller, 'Pervasive Label Errors in Test Sets Destabilize ML Benchmarks' (NeurIPS 2021)
Liu et al., CelebA paper (ICCV 2015)
Lingenfelter et al., 'A Quantitative Analysis of Labeling Issues in the CelebA Dataset' (ISVC 2022)
